extension_name: Devinization Pack 3 — Prompt, Memory & Context Architecture
purpose: Define the prompt, memory, recall, context, archive, and continuity layer that lets the Station Chief and future agent runtime preserve context, use the right instructions, recall the right project state, avoid context soup, separate memory types, archive important decisions, transfer useful patterns across projects, and resume work without losing the plot.
core_operating_philosophy: The Agent Command Center remains a massive command civilization with selective live deployment. The whole 175-family agency remains available for awareness, routing, governance, memory, and context. This pack does not shrink the art piece. This pack defines how the system remembers, retrieves, prompts, focuses, archives, and carries context forward without turning every task into a haunted junk drawer with a README.
activation_rule: Use this pack when the user says or implies: prompt system, instruction architecture, memory, remember this, recall context, context architecture, don’t lose this, thread move, archive this, save this, vault, shelf, master archive, blueberry pancakes, active project state, cross-project pattern, context soup, conversation design, attention management, cognitive workspace, prompt template, Station Chief prompt, worker prompt, resume from context, use the right memory, preserve continuity.

memory_layers.name: Immediate Conversation Context
memory_layers.purpose: Use current-turn and recent-turn information before asking repeated questions.

memory_layers.name: Active Project State
memory_layers.purpose: Track current project, current phase, active files, open tasks, blocked items, and next action.

memory_layers.name: User Preference Memory
memory_layers.purpose: Apply Devin’s formatting, tone, execution, shortcut, and workflow preferences.

memory_layers.name: Command Language Memory
memory_layers.purpose: Resolve shorthand commands, code phrases, modes, and trigger phrases.

memory_layers.name: Project Archive Memory
memory_layers.purpose: Store durable project decisions, corrections, lock reports, baseline states, and implementation notes.

memory_layers.name: Cross-Project Pattern Memory
memory_layers.purpose: Transfer lessons, structures, failure patterns, and reusable approaches across projects.

memory_layers.name: Master Archive Memory
memory_layers.purpose: Maintain long-term continuity, searchable archive paths, and retrieval rules across the full Agent Command Center.

global_rules: 1. Preserve the locked 175-family baseline unless the user explicitly authorizes baseline mutation.
2. Use overlays for Devinization unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
3. Do not treat memory as one bucket.
4. Separate conversation context, active project state, user preferences, command language, project archive, cross-project patterns, and master archive memory.
5. Do not ask repeated questions when the answer already exists in context.
6. Treat “blueberry pancakes” as a remember/lock-state signal unless explicitly overridden.
7. Treat “check please” as a verification command and recall the active task pointer before answering.
8. When moving threads, create a compact continuity bridge before continuing.
9. Every long-running task must have a resumable context packet.
10. Prompt templates must be role-specific and task-specific.
11. Prompt templates must include scope, allowed actions, forbidden actions, required outputs, and validation requirements.
12. Archive decisions, corrections, validator results, and final lock reports.
13. Do not flood working context with the entire archive.
14. Retrieve only the relevant archive slices for the active task.
15. Use cross-project lessons when useful, but do not override current task instructions.
16. Preserve the art piece: the whole agency remains available for memory, recall, governance, and context, while live execution remains budgeted.

crews.mission: Define the core Station Chief prompt architecture so one user command can be interpreted, scoped, routed, checked, and reported without losing Devin’s intent.
Create reusable prompt templates for Station Chief, family chiefs, department chiefs, workers, scribes, auditors, and final-lock reviewers.
Ensure every executable prompt includes objective, context, scope, allowed actions, forbidden actions, required outputs, validators, and stop conditions.
Review prompts for ambiguity, scope drift, missing outputs, missing validation, overbroad execution, and unnecessary credential stalls.
Track prompt versions, changes, stable baselines, prompt experiments, and deprecated prompt patterns.
Organize prompt templates into a searchable library by role, task type, mode, project, and execution risk.
handoff_targets: DV.P3.001 Station Chief Prompt Architecture Crew

crews.mission: Define how immediate context, active project state, user preferences, command language, project archives, cross-project lessons, and master archive memory remain separated but connected.
Maintain the current project state, active phase, open tasks, changed files, blockers, validator status, and next action.
Compress long context into compact, accurate continuation packets without losing constraints, decisions, file paths, or unresolved issues.
Inject only relevant context into prompts, avoiding archive flooding and irrelevant memory clutter.
Add validated memory snippets into active prompts with source, purpose, confidence, and scope tags.
Resolve conflicts between old memory, new instruction, project-specific rules, strict mode, and current task constraints.
Decide whether a memory should be recalled, withheld, summarized, ignored, or escalated based on relevance, sensitivity, and task scope.
Filter recalled memory so the system retrieves the useful slice instead of dumping the whole archive into active context.
Mark memory as current, stale, superseded, uncertain, locked, or requires verification before use.
Lock important state, decisions, user rules, baseline reports, and correction history so future runs do not overwrite or forget them casually.
Mark irrelevant, wrong, superseded, or harmful memory as ignored for active workflows without damaging archive history.
Audit memory use for relevance, correctness, scope, source, and whether it caused bad assumptions or unnecessary repetition.
Preserve task continuity across “ok,” “next,” “check please,” interruptions, new threads, and long gaps.
Choose the correct dialogue mode: answer, verify, prompt-write, build, repair, remember-only, explain, or ask-one-question.
Avoid asking unnecessary questions when context already contains enough information to proceed safely.
Shape responses according to Devin’s needs: direct, copy/paste-ready, detailed when requested, no filler, and clear proof when checking work.
Prevent the system from switching from explanation to execution, from check to build, or from prompt-writing to file mutation without user intent.
Detect likely response misfires before they happen, including overexplaining, wrong task mode, missing proof, assuming too much, or ignoring strict instructions.
Identify reusable patterns from prior projects and apply them to current tasks without overriding current instructions.
Extract lessons from completed projects, corrections, failures, lock reports, and validator history.
Identify structures that can be reused across prompts, reports, validators, runtime files, overlays, and project documentation.
Track repeated failure patterns such as partial completion, wrong validator counts, scope drift, placeholder reports, and missing commit evidence.
Compare a new task to prior projects and identify helpful similarities, risks, reusable assets, and traps.
Prevent cross-project memory from distracting the current task by filtering only the relevant lesson or structure.
Maintain an active workspace index of the current task, active project, active pack, current files, next action, and blocked items.
Prevent shiny-object drift, unrelated improvements, unnecessary tool use, and switching projects without explicit instruction.
Clear stale active items, obsolete context, completed tasks, and duplicate notes from active working memory while preserving archive history.
Restore focus after interruption, model limit, thread shift, tool failure, or context overload.
Ensure transferred patterns support the current task rather than hijacking it with irrelevant prior-project logic.
Manage active context size so the system preserves the right context without overloading prompts, model calls, or runtime memory.
Decide what should be archived into personal/project shelves, what should stay active, and what should be ignored.
Define vaults, shelves, cabinets, project folders, save piles, and retrieval paths for durable stored knowledge.
Classify archive items by project, type, durability, relevance, sensitivity, and future retrieval trigger.
Create retrieval pathways so saved knowledge can be found by phrase, project, family, pack, date, task type, or trigger.
Archive compact continuity records for long-running work so future runs can resume from a clean bridge instead of rereading everything.
Mark archive items by access boundary, privacy sensitivity, user-only scope, project-only scope, or safe-to-use-in-output status.
Link related historical threads, correction sequences, lock reports, prompts, project states, and implementation milestones.
Maintain the master catalog of durable assets, overlays, packs, reports, validators, runtime milestones, and archive shelves.
Preserve source-of-truth records, commit hashes, validator outputs, user decisions, correction prompts, and final lock artifacts.
Verify that retrieved archive items are relevant, current, correctly scoped, and not misleading before they enter active context.
Lock completed continuity milestones so future work can resume from validated baseline states rather than informal memory.
Serve as the master librarian for the Agent Command Center, deciding where knowledge belongs, how it is found, and how it stays usable without flooding active work.

ownership_metadata.ownership_phrase: Project owner, system architect, and operating-doctrine author: Devin O’Rourke.
ownership_metadata.applies_to: Agent Command Center Devinization overlay architecture
ownership_metadata.baseline_note: The locked 175-family baseline remains preserved; this metadata identifies Devin O’Rourke as owner/architect of the Devinization overlay stack.
